content:
# Machinery Malfunctions

Using the available data, identify which technicians are responsible for maintenance-caused quality rejections, and list the products affected by each such technician.

Context:
- Machines produce batches over time and carry a persistent technical condition that is not directly recorded in any table.
- A maintenance event may improve the machine, leave it unchanged, or introduce a hidden fault. Hidden faults are never labeled as such in the data.
- A hidden fault affects later batches produced on the same machine until a subsequent maintenance event changes the machine state.
- A given fault class may affect only some products. A technician can therefore be responsible for quality issues on one product while leaving other products on the same machine unaffected.
- Quality inspection happens with a delay, so inspection date is not the same as production date.
- Defects also arise from unrelated factors and natural variation that must be ruled out.

Linking a rejected batch to a maintenance event:
A batch can be attributed to one specific maintenance event only when all three hold:
  a) the batch was produced on the machine that the technician serviced,
  b) production started after that maintenance was completed,
  c) production started before the next maintenance event on the same machine.

Confirming a technician:
A technician is confirmed only if their own maintenance work can be linked to at least 3 distinct rejected batches, distributed across at least 2 different work orders performed by that same technician.
Technicians below either threshold are isolated cases and must be excluded.

Reporting products for a confirmed technician:
Once a technician is confirmed, include every product for which at least one rejected batch can be causally linked to that technician's faulty maintenance - even if that product-technician pair has only one supporting batch.
Do not include products whose rejections are explained by non-maintenance factors, or for which no link to a confirmed technician exists.

Dataset:
- The generated CSV files are available directly in `/app/`.
- Raw service reports are available under `/app/raw_service_reports/`.

Answer format:
- Write your final answer to `/app/answer.json` using JSON format.
- Use product IDs as object keys.
- Use arrays of technician IDs as values.
- Sort product IDs ascending.
- Sort technician IDs ascending inside each array.
- Omit products with no confirmed responsible technician.

Example:
```json
{"P_004": ["TECH_013", "TECH_016"], "P_005": ["TECH_001"]}
```

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

import pandas as pd, re, glob, os

rows = []
for path in glob.glob('raw_service_reports/day_*/*.txt'):
    txt = open(path).read()
    d = dict(re.findall(r'^([A-Z ]+):\s*(.+)$', txt, re.M))
    rows.append({
        'day': int(d['DAY']), 'wo': d['WORK ORDER'], 'machine_id': d['MACHINE'],
        'tech': d['TECHNICIAN'], 'work_type': d['WORK TYPE'], 'reason': d['REPORTED REASON'],
        'started': d['STARTED'], 'completed': d['COMPLETED'], 'status': d['FINAL STATUS']})
mw = pd.DataFrame(rows)
print(len(mw), "maintenance events parsed")
mw.to_csv('/tmp/maintenance_parsed.csv', index=False)

def tmin(s): h,m = s.split(':'); return int(h)*60+int(m)
mw['comp_min'] = mw['completed'].map(tmin)
mw['start_min'] = mw['started'].map(tmin)
print("maintenance start time range:", mw.start_min.min()/60, mw.start_min.max()/60)
print("maintenance completion time range:", mw.comp_min.min()/60, mw.comp_min.max()/60)
print("any completed < started (overnight)?", (mw.comp_min < mw.start_min).sum())

mal = pd.read_csv('machine_assignment_log.csv')
mal['s_min'] = mal['start_time'].map(tmin)
print("production start time range:", mal.s_min.min()/60, mal.s_min.max()/60)
print("events per machine:", mw.groupby('machine_id').size().describe())
EOF

python3 << 'EOF'
import pandas as pd, numpy as np

mw = pd.read_csv('/tmp/maintenance_parsed.csv')
# multiple events same machine same day?
dup = mw.groupby(['machine_id','day']).size()
print("machine-days with >1 maintenance:", (dup>1).sum())

pb = pd.read_csv('production_batches.csv')
qi = pd.read_csv('quality_inspections.csv')
df = pb.merge(qi[['batch_id','rejected_pct','defect_code','inspection_result','inspection_day']], on='batch_id')
df['rejected'] = (df.inspection_result=='REJECTED').astype(int)

# order maintenance per machine by (day, completed time)
def tmin(s): h,m=s.split(':'); return int(h)*60+int(m)
mw['comp_min'] = mw['completed'].map(tmin)
mw = mw.sort_values(['machine_id','day','comp_min']).reset_index(drop=True)
mw['evt_idx'] = mw.groupby('machine_id').cumcount()

# for each machine, maintenance "effective day" = day (completes before production that day)
# attribute batch on (machine, prod_day) to last event with event day <= prod_day (last completed that morning or earlier)
import bisect
ev_by_machine = {m: g[['day','wo','tech','work_type','reason','evt_idx']].values.tolist()
                 for m,g in mw.groupby('machine_id')}
ev_days = {m: [r[0] for r in v] for m,v in ev_by_machine.items()}

def attribute(row):
    m, d = row['machine_id'], row['production_day']
    days = ev_days.get(m)
    if not days: return None
    i = bisect.bisect_right(days, d) - 1
    if i < 0: return None
    return ev_by_machine[m][i][1]  # work order id

df['attr_wo'] = df.apply(attribute, axis=1)
print("batches attributed:", df.attr_wo.notna().sum(), "of", len(df))
df.to_csv('/tmp/batches_attributed.csv', index=False)

# rejections per attributed work order
rej = df[df.rejected==1]
per_wo = rej.groupby('attr_wo').size().sort_values(ascending=False)
print("\nrejected batches per WO interval (top 30):")
print(per_wo.head(30))
print("\nWOs with >=2 rejections:", (per_wo>=2).sum(), " | with 1:", (per_wo==1).sum())
EOF

import pandas as pd, numpy as np
from scipy.stats import binom
pd.set_option('display.width', 250); pd.set_option('display.max_rows', 200)

df = pd.read_csv('/tmp/batches_attributed.csv')
prod = pd.read_csv('products.csv')
mw = pd.read_csv('/tmp/maintenance_parsed.csv')
df = df.merge(prod[['product_id','product_family','quality_rejection_threshold_pct']], on='product_id')
df['ratio'] = df.rejected_pct / df.quality_rejection_threshold_pct

# baseline per product (will include faults, slight overestimate -> conservative)
base = df.groupby('product_id').rejected.mean().rename('p0')
print("baseline rejection rate per product:"); print(base.sort_values(ascending=False).head(10))

g = df.groupby(['attr_wo','product_id']).agg(
    n=('rejected','size'), k=('rejected','sum'),
    mean_ratio_rej=('ratio', lambda s: np.nan),
).reset_index()
mr = df[df.rejected==1].groupby(['attr_wo','product_id']).ratio.mean().rename('mean_ratio_rej').reset_index()
g = g.drop(columns='mean_ratio_rej').merge(mr, on=['attr_wo','product_id'], how='left')
g = g.merge(base, on='product_id')
g['pval'] = 1 - binom.cdf(g.k-1, g.n, g.p0)
g = g.merge(mw[['wo','machine_id','tech','work_type','reason','day']], left_on='attr_wo', right_on='wo')

cand = g[(g.k>=2)].sort_values('pval')
print("\n(interval, product) clusters with k>=2 rejections:")
print(cand[['attr_wo','day','machine_id','tech','work_type','product_id','n','k','mean_ratio_rej','pval']].to_string(index=False))
g.to_csv('/tmp/wo_product_stats.csv', index=False)

def timeline(machine, d0, d1):

    for _,r in rj.iterrows():
        items.append((r.production_day, 1, f"   REJ {r.batch_id} {r.product_id} ratio={r.ratio} (attr {r.attr_wo})"))
    for d,_,s in sorted(items):
        print(f"d{d:3d} {s}")
    print()

input.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import pandas as pd, numpy as np
df = pd.read_csv('/tmp/batches_attributed.csv')
prod = pd.read_csv('products.csv')
cond = pd.read_csv('daily_factory_conditions.csv')
jobs = pd.read_csv('production_jobs.csv')
df = df.merge(prod[['product_id','quality_rejection_threshold_pct']], on='product_id')
df = df.merge(cond, left_on='production_day', right_on='day')
df = df.merge(jobs[['job_id','job_difficulty']], on='job_id')
df['lpct'] = np.log(df.rejected_pct.clip(lower=1e-4))

# regression on accepted batches: product FE + difficulty FE + day indices
X = pd.get_dummies(df[['product_id']], dtype=float)
X = pd.concat([X, pd.get_dummies(df.job_difficulty.astype(str), prefix='diff', dtype=float),
               df[['load_index','humidity_index','material_variation_index','power_stability_index']]], axis=1)
acc_mask = (df.inspection_result=='ACCEPTED').values
import numpy.linalg as la
Xa, ya = X.values[acc_mask], df.lpct.values[acc_mask]
coef, *_ = la.lstsq(Xa, ya, rcond=None)
pred = X.values @ coef
df['resid'] = df.lpct - pred
sd = df.resid[acc_mask].std()
print("residual sd (accepted):", sd.round(3))
df['z'] = df.resid/sd

rej = df[df.inspection_result=='REJECTED']
h = np.histogram(rej.z, bins=[0,1,1.5,2,2.5,3,3.5,4,5,6,8,12])
print("z histogram for REJECTED:", dict(zip(['0-1','1-1.5','1.5-2','2-2.5','2.5-3','3-3.5','3.5-4','4-5','5-6','6-8','8-12'], h[0])))
print("rejected with z<0:", (rej.z<0).sum())
ha = np.histogram(df.z[acc_mask], bins=[-10,-3,-2,-1,0,1,2,3,4,10])
print("z histogram ACCEPTED:", dict(zip(['<-3','-3--2','-2--1','-1-0','0-1','1-2','2-3','3-4','>4'], ha[0])))
df[['batch_id','production_day','machine_id','product_id','attr_wo','rejected_pct','quality_rejection_threshold_pct','inspection_result','z']].to_csv('/tmp/batches_model.csv', index=False)
EOF

import pandas as pd, numpy as np
pd.set_option('display.width', 250); pd.set_option('display.max_rows', 500)
bm = pd.read_csv('/tmp/batches_model.csv')
mw = pd.read_csv('/tmp/maintenance_parsed.csv')

rej = bm[(bm.inspection_result=='REJECTED')].copy()
hi = rej[rej.z>=2.5].sort_values(['machine_id','production_day'])
print("rejected batches with z>=2.5:", len(hi))
# group into episodes per machine: gap of <= 12 days between consecutive high-z rejections
eps = []
for m, g in hi.groupby('machine_id'):
    g = g.sort_values('production_day')
    cur = []
    last = None
    for _, r in g.iterrows():
        if last is not None and r.production_day - last > 12:
            eps.append((m, cur)); cur = []
        cur.append(r); last = r.production_day
    if cur: eps.append((m, cur))
print("episodes:", len(eps))
for m, batches in eps:
    d0, d1 = batches[0].production_day, batches[-1].production_day
    prods = sorted(set(b.product_id for b in batches))
    wos = sorted(set(b.attr_wo for b in batches))
    first_wo = batches[0].attr_wo
    t0 = mw[mw.wo==first_wo].iloc[0]
    print(f"{m} days {d0}-{d1}: {len(batches)} batches, products {prods}, intro WO {first_wo} d{t0.day} {t0.tech} {t0.work_type}; WOs spanned: {wos}")
EOF

import pandas as pd, numpy as np
pd.set_option('display.width', 250); pd.set_option('display.max_rows', 300)
bm = pd.read_csv('/tmp/batches_model.csv')
mw = pd.read_csv('/tmp/maintenance_parsed.csv')
bm['rej'] = (bm.inspection_result=='REJECTED').astype(int)

g = bm.groupby(['attr_wo','product_id']).agg(n=('z','size'), k=('rej','sum'),
    mean_z=('z','mean'), max_z=('z','max')).reset_index()
g['t'] = g.mean_z*np.sqrt(g.n)
g = g.merge(mw[['wo','machine_id','tech','work_type','day']], left_on='attr_wo', right_on='wo')

# distribution of t for groups with no rejection (null)
null = g[g.k==0]
print("null t: mean %.2f sd %.2f, q99 %.2f q999 %.2f max %.2f" % (null.t.mean(), null.t.std(), null.t.quantile(0.99), null.t.quantile(0.999), null.t.max()))

cand = g[(g.k>=1)].sort_values('t', ascending=False)
print("\ntop 60 (interval,product) by t (only k>=1):")
print(cand.head(60)[['attr_wo','day','machine_id','tech','work_type','product_id','n','k','mean_z','max_z','t']].round(2).to_string(index=False))
print("\nt histogram for k>=1 groups:", np.histogram(cand.t, bins=[-5,0,1,2,3,4,5,6,8,10,15,25])[0])
g.to_csv('/tmp/wo_product_z.csv', index=False)

df = df.merge(jobs[['job_id','job_difficulty']], on='job_id')
df['lpct'] = np.log(df.rejected_pct.clip(lower=1e-4))

# model WITH machine FE, fit on accepted only
X = pd.concat([pd.get_dummies(df.product_id, dtype=float),
               pd.get_dummies(df.machine_id, dtype=float),
               pd.get_dummies(df.job_difficulty.astype(str), prefix='diff', dtype=float),
               df[['load_index','humidity_index','material_variation_index','power_stability_index']]], axis=1)
m = (df.inspection_result=='ACCEPTED').values
import numpy.linalg as la
coef,*_ = la.lstsq(X.values[m], df.lpct.values[m], rcond=None)
df['z2'] = (df.lpct - X.values@coef)/ (df.lpct - X.values@coef)[m].std()
print("machine-FE model: resid sd accepted:", (df.lpct - X.values@coef)[m].std().round(3))

# P_010 z2 mean per machine

python3 << 'EOF'
import pandas as pd, numpy as np
import numpy.linalg as la
pd.set_option('display.width', 250); pd.set_option('display.max_rows', 400)
df = pd.read_csv('/tmp/batches_full.csv')
df['lpct'] = np.log(df.rejected_pct.clip(lower=1e-4))

X = pd.concat([pd.get_dummies(df.product_id, dtype=float),
               pd.get_dummies(df.operator_id, dtype=float),
               pd.get_dummies(df.job_difficulty.astype(str), prefix='diff', dtype=float),
               df[['load_index','humidity_index','material_variation_index','power_stability_index']]], axis=1)
m = (df.inspection_result=='ACCEPTED').values
coef,*_ = la.lstsq(X.values[m], df.lpct.values[m], rcond=None)
resid = df.lpct - X.values@coef
sd = resid[m].std()
df['z'] = resid/sd
print("resid sd:", sd.round(3), "| accepted z>3:", ((df.z>3)&m).sum(), "max acc z:", df.z[m].max().round(2))

df['rej'] = (df.inspection_result=='REJECTED').astype(int)
g = df.groupby(['attr_wo','product_id']).agg(n=('z','size'), k=('rej','sum'), mean_z=('z','mean'), max_z=('z','max')).reset_index()
g['t'] = g.mean_z*np.sqrt(g.n)
mw = pd.read_csv('/tmp/maintenance_parsed.csv')
g = g.merge(mw[['wo','machine_id','tech','work_type','day']], left_on='attr_wo', right_on='wo')
null = g[g.k==0]
print("null t: q999 %.2f max %.2f (n=%d)" % (null.t.quantile(0.999), null.t.max(), len(null)))
cand = g[g.k>=1].sort_values('t', ascending=False)
print("\nk>=1 groups, t>=2.8:")
print(cand[cand.t>=2.8][['attr_wo','day','machine_id','tech','work_type','product_id','n','k','mean_z','max_z','t']].round(2).to_string(index=False))
df.to_csv('/tmp/batches_v2.csv', index=False)
g.to_csv('/tmp/groups_v2.csv', index=False)
EOF

fb = fb[fb.z>=2]

fb = fb.merge(mw[['wo','tech']], left_on='attr_wo', right_on='wo')

content.text:
Done — the answer is written to `/app/answer.json`. Eight technicians are confirmed, affecting eleven products.

## How I got there

**Pipeline.** I parsed all 3,074 raw service reports (uniform format: machine, technician, work type, start/completion time). Maintenance always finishes by 05:30 and production starts at 06:00+, so each batch links unambiguously to the most recent maintenance event on its machine (conditions a/b/c reduce to "the immediately preceding event"). I joined batches with quality inspections via `batch_id`, so inspection delay was not an issue.

**Ruling out natural variation.** Of 342 rejected batches, most are marginal (reject % barely over threshold). I modeled log(rejected_pct) on product, job difficulty, daily factory conditions, and operator — operators mattered: LOW-experience operators run ~0.3 higher in log terms, which fully explained an apparent P_010 cluster on M_007 (days 87–98, all on LOW-experience days; this was the deciding factor that ruled out TECH_041). Residual z-scores split rejections into a natural tail (z < ~3.4, matching the accepted-batch distribution) and a fault population (z 4–8). At the interval×product level, a t-statistic over all batches gave clean separation: 11,598 fault-free groups max out at t=3.67, while fault groups run t≈4–15.

**Fault signature.** Faults are product-specific (e.g., on M_056 all five P_015 batches rejected at 1.9–4.6× threshold while other products in the same interval were untouched) and persist until a later maintenance fixes them — verified explicitly on M_028: clean before TECH_004's day-309 reset, 100% P_004 rejection (z 5–7) through two ineffective services, clean immediately after a day-317 corrective.

**Confirmation thresholds.** Applying ≥3 linked fault batches across ≥2 work orders: confirmed are TECH_002, TECH_003, TECH_014, TECH_021, TECH_027, TECH_030, TECH_031, TECH_037. Notable exclusions: TECH_010 (5 rejected batches, but all on one work order) and TECH_004 (3 batches, one work order) fail the two-work-order rule; TECH_041, TECH_023, TECH_033, TECH_017, TECH_035 and others have only 1–2 linkable batches. Products whose rejections were purely natural variation (P_010, P_020, P_021, P_017, P_002…) or linked only to unconfirmed technicians (P_011, P_016) are omitted.

| Product | Confirmed technicians |
|---|---|
| P_001 | TECH_014, TECH_027, TECH_031 |
| P_004 | TECH_003, TECH_030 |
| P_006 | TECH_002 |
| P_008 | TECH_031 |
| P_015 | TECH_014, TECH_021 |
| P_018 | TECH_014 |
| P_019 | TECH_030 |
| P_024 | TECH_003, TECH_014, TECH_027, TECH_037 |
| P_026 | TECH_014, TECH_031 |
| P_027 | TECH_002, TECH_021 |
| P_029 | TECH_002, TECH_003, TECH_014, TECH_027, TECH_037 |

One judgment call worth flagging: faults sometimes persist through a later technician's ineffective service, and the linking rule (condition c) attributes batches produced after that service to the later technician. I applied the rule as written. This is what confirms TECH_030 (3 P_019 batches from their own fault on M_009, plus 2 P_004 batches following their ineffective calibration on the already-faulty M_028) and adds the single-batch product links P_024→TECH_014/TECH_027 and P_029→TECH_037; the wording "even if that product-technician pair has only one supporting batch" suggests these inclusions are intended.
